title: Germany’s Hidden Burden: Healthcare-Associated Infections Compared to Europe
text:
Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) are illnesses acquired during hospital care. They increase treatment costs, prolong hospital stays, and may lead to disability or death.
Although Germany’s hospital prevalence of HAIs is lower than the EU/EEA average, the population-level burden is higher. This apparent paradox arises from Germany’s high hospital admission rates and longer average lengths of stay.
This blog post analyses data from the ECDC Point Prevalence Survey (2011 - 2012) to explain Germany’s elevated burden and identify which infection types matter most. The five HAI types considered account for ≈79% of HAIs in the PPS frame:
- Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)
- Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP)
- Bloodstream infection (BSI)
- Urinary tract infection (UTI)
- Surgical site infection (SSI)

title: Germany’s Hidden Burden: Healthcare-Associated Infections Compared to Europe
text:
Data source
This analysis draws on the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC) Point Prevalence Survey, 2011–2012. Burden estimates were calculated using the BCoDE methodology (Burden of Communicable Diseases in Europe), implemented in the BHAI R package (Willrich, Zacher, and Haller 2019).

title: Germany’s Hidden Burden: Healthcare-Associated Infections Compared to Europe
text:
Germany vs EU/EEA
Table 1 shows annual cases, deaths, and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) per 100,000 population. Germany exceeds the EU/EEA average in all measures.


Code
burden <- data.frame(
  Metric   = c("Cases (per 100k)", "Deaths (per 100k)", "DALYs (per 100k)"),
  Germany  = c(592, 20.1, 308),
  EU_EEA   = c(468, 15.6, 290)
)

knitr::kable(
  burden,
  caption = NULL,
  align = c("l","r","r"),
  booktabs = TRUE
)




Table 1: Estimated HAI burden per 100,000 population, Germany vs EU/EEA (2011–2012)






Metric
Germany
EU_EEA




Cases (per 100k)
592.0
468.0


Deaths (per 100k)
20.1
15.6


DALYs (per 100k)
308.0
290.0








Figure 1 displays DALYs for major HAI types in Germany. Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP) and bloodstream infections (BSI) dominate mortality, while urinary tract infections (UTI) and surgical site infections (SSI) contribute to disability.


Code
library(ggplot2)

types_totals <- data.frame(
  Infection = c("HAP","BSI","UTI","SSI","CDI"),
  DALYs = c(69508, 58350, 66701, 28842, 20890)
)

ggplot(types_totals, aes(x = Infection, y = DALYs)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#274060") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(DALYs)),
            vjust = -0.35, size = 3.5) +
  labs(title = "DALYs by infection type in Germany (absolute)",
       x = "Infection type",
       y = "DALYs (years)",
       caption = "Source: ECDC PPS 2011–2012; BHAI estimates (German PPS sample).") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#005c97"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 10, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )








Figure 1: Germany’s HAI burden by infection type (DALYs, German PPS 2011–2012)





Table 2 breaks this down by infection type.
- Germany’s case rate for UTI is ~1.5× higher than the EU/EEA average (265 vs 175 per 100 k).
- CDI and BSI show notably higher fatality and DALY rates.
- HAP rates are slightly lower in Germany, yet the absolute burden remains large due to long stays and recurrent pneumonia among older inpatients.


Code
types_rates <- data.frame(
  Infection = c("HAP","UTI","BSI","SSI","CDI","ALL"),
  Germany_cases = c(132.0, 265.1, 33.4, 115.4, 44.6, 592.1),
  EU_EEA_cases  = c(143.7, 174.7, 22.2, 111.3, 16.0, 467.9),
  Germany_deaths = c(4.9, 4.5, 4.8, 2.9, 2.4, 20.1),
  EU_EEA_deaths  = c(5.3, 3.0, 3.3, 2.6, 0.9, 15.3),
  Germany_DALYs  = c(86.1, 82.6, 72.2, 35.7, 25.9, 308.2),
  EU_EEA_DALYs   = c(109.8, 57.1, 76.2, 35.1, 10.0, 290.0)
)

knitr::kable(
  types_rates,
  caption = NULL,
  align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r","r"),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  col.names = c("Infection","Cases DE","Cases EU","Deaths DE","Deaths EU","DALYs DE","DALYs EU")
)




Table 2: HAI burden per 100,000 by infection type: Germany (PPS) vs EU/EEA (ECDC PPS), 2011–2012

Together, these tables demonstrate that Germany’s excess burden stems not from one pathogen but from system-wide exposure and patient throughput: more admissions and longer hospital stays amplify even modest infection risks.

title: Germany’s Hidden Burden: Healthcare-Associated Infections Compared to Europe
text:
Findings

Germany had ≈478,000 cases, 16,000 deaths, and 249,000 DALYs from HAIs in 2011–2012
HAP and BSI caused more than half the total burden despite fewer cases.
UTI was the most common infection but had lower fatality.
SSI contributed disproportionately to disability.
HAIs imposed a greater health burden than influenza, salmonella, hepatitis B, and measles combined

These findings link back to the problem statement: Germany’s system-level hospital practices amplify the burden despite lower prevalence.

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
This project turned raw, messy operational data from a fictional equipment company (MonEquip) into a fully functioning business intelligence data warehouse — complete with cleaned facts, conformed dimensions, and actionable KPIs.
The dataset covered sales and hire transactions from April 2018 to December 2020.

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
This project turned raw, messy operational data from a fictional equipment company (MonEquip) into a fully functioning business intelligence data warehouse — complete with cleaned facts, conformed dimensions, and actionable KPIs.
The dataset covered sales and hire transactions from April 2018 to December 2020.

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
The Problem: Dirty Data
Before any analysis could happen, the raw data needed serious cleaning. I identified and resolved 6 types of data errors:







Error Type
Description




Negative quantities
Sales rows with quantity ≤ 0


Negative/zero prices
Hire rows with invalid total prices


Date inconsistencies
END_DATE before START_DATE


Duplicate records
CUSTOMER_ID appearing multiple times


Orphan foreign keys
Hire rows referencing missing customers, equipment, or staff


Mismatched totals
TOTAL_SALES_PRICE ≠ QUANTITY × UNIT_SALES_PRICE



Each error type was quarantined into a dedicated issue table for audit traceability, then clean versions were built excluding invalid rows.

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
Star Schema Design
I designed a multi-fact constellation (galaxy) schema with two primary fact tables sharing conformed dimensions:
Fact Tables:

SalesFact — one row per sales transaction line
HireFact — one row per hire transaction line

Shared Dimensions:

TimeDim — pre-computed Year, Month, Season for trend analysis
BranchDim — company branch locations
CategoryDim — equipment categories (Earthmoving, Landscaping, etc.)
CustomerTypeDim — Business vs Individual customers

Sales-only Dimension:

SalesPriceScaleDim — Low / Medium / High price buckets

All fact-dimension joins were validated with zero missing keys before analysis.

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
Key Findings

Revenue Overview (2018–2020)



Metric
Sales
Hire




Total Revenue
$11.58M
$0.14M


Avg per Transaction
$77,220
$470


Units
393 sold
617 hired



Sales completely dominate revenue. Hire generates many small, short transactions.


Top Categories
Earthmoving (17.6%) and Landscaping (13.3%) together account for over 30% of total sales revenue — the company’s core strength lies in heavy construction equipment.


Branch Performance
Clayton branch leads with nearly $2.8M in revenue — more than double Parkville ($1.45M). Clayton has a diverse portfolio across almost all categories and acts as a central revenue hub.


Seasonality

Sales peak in Summer and Spring, with a broad step-change growth of +57.5% in 2020
Hire peaks in Spring and Autumn — aligned with project start and finish windows
Same-day hires account for only 9.9% of hire revenue; multi-day rentals dominate



Year-over-Year Growth



Period
Growth




2018 → 2019
+2.4%


2019 → 2020
+57.5%



The 2020 jump was broad-based across all months and seasons — suggesting a structural shift in demand rather than a one-off event.

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
Visualisation
The final analytics were visualised as a one-page poster in R, combining a star schema diagram, bar charts, donut charts, and a monthly revenue trend line.



Sales & Hire Analytics Poster

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
Tools Used
Oracle SQL · R · ggplot2 · Lucidchart · DBeaver · Star Schema Design

title: Sales & Hire Analytics: Building a Data Warehouse from Scratch
text:
Takeaways
This project taught me how real-world data is never clean, and how important it is to build auditable cleaning pipelines before touching any analysis. Designing the star schema also showed me how grain decisions — what one row represents — fundamentally shape what questions you can and can’t answer downstream.

title: Nhu Nguyen
text:
Melbourne, Australia


LinkedIn · GitHub · nguyenthunhu2603@gmail.com


I’m a Master’s student in Business Analytics at Monash University, where I’m building toward a career that blends data analysis, logistics, and strategy. My background spans international logistics and supply chain operations, and over time I’ve grown increasingly focused on how data can tell better stories and drive smarter decisions. I work mainly with SQL, R, and Python to turn raw, messy data into clear insights and simple predictive models that actually help things move, and move faster.
I like clean processes, reproducible workflows, and work that feels structured but creative. My experience ranges from coordinating sea freight operations and building reporting prototypes to leading small project teams and solving real-time logistics issues.

Workflow tools: Quarto, R Markdown, Git/GitHub

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text: How well does Melbourne’s bus network actually serve its residents? This project investigates that question using real public transport and census data by combining SQL spatial analysis with GIS visualisation to uncover where the gaps are.

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text: How well does Melbourne’s bus network actually serve its residents? This project investigates that question using real public transport and census data by combining SQL spatial analysis with GIS visualisation to uncover where the gaps are.

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text:
Data Sources

PTV GTFS Data — routes, stops, trips, and timetables from the Public Transport Victoria Open Data Portal
ABS Mesh Block Boundaries — 2021 census geographic boundaries covering all of Metropolitan Melbourne

Both datasets were loaded into PostgreSQL (with the PostGIS spatial extension) using a Docker environment managed through DBeaver and QGIS.

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text:
What I Did

Data Cleaning & Preprocessing

Imported 11 core tables into a dedicated ptv schema in PostgreSQL
Added geometry columns to the stops table using latitude/longitude coordinates (GDA2020 projection, SRID 7844)
Filtered to bus-only routes (route_type = 3) and residential mesh blocks (MB_CAT21 = 'Residential')
Created a Greater Melbourne subset of 59,483 mesh blocks



Spatial Analysis
Using ST_Intersects, I spatially joined each residential mesh block with bus stop locations to count how many stops fell within each block boundary. This produced a derived table (bus_stops_per_block) with stop counts per block.
I also identified residential areas with zero bus stops — stored as residential_no_bus, to highlight the most underserved zones.


Visualisation
A heatmap was built in QGIS using the Viridis colour ramp with Natural Breaks (Jenks) classification:



Accessibility Level
Stop Count




No Access
0 stops


Low Access
1–2 stops


Moderate Access
3–5 stops


High Access
6–10 stops


Very High Access
10+ stops






Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne — Residential Focus

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text:
Key Findings
Out of 46,608 total residential mesh blocks in Metropolitan Melbourne:

Only 11,264 (24.17%) contain at least one bus stop
76% of residential areas have no direct bus access
The average stop count is just 0.43 stops per block
Dense coverage is concentrated in inner suburbs (Carlton, Richmond, Brunswick, South Yarra)
Outer growth corridors — Melton, Clyde North, Wyndham Vale — are heavily underserved

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text:
Spatial Pattern
The heatmap reveals a clear radial accessibility gradient: service density decreases steadily with distance from the CBD. Melbourne’s transport network is historically CBD-centric, and outer suburbs that depend on buses are the most disadvantaged.

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text:
Implications
These findings highlight a significant transport equity gap. Outer and newly developed suburbs lack reliable bus connectivity despite rapid population growth. The Victorian Government’s Bus Reform Plan (2023) identifies similar gaps — this analysis provides spatial evidence to support targeted network expansion.

title: Bus Accessibility in Metropolitan Melbourne
text:
Tools Used
PostgreSQL · PostGIS · QGIS · SQL · Docker · DBeaver

title: Germany’s Hidden Burden: Healthcare-Associated Infections Compared to Europe
text:
Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) are illnesses patients acquire during hospital care. They extend hospital stays, increase costs, and can lead to disability or death. Although Germany’s hospitals report lower infection prevalence than the EU/EEA average, the population-level burden is higher, largely due to high admission rates and longer lengths of stay.
This post compares Germany’s HAI burden with the EU/EEA and highlights which infection types matter most.

title: Germany’s Hidden Burden: Healthcare-Associated Infections Compared to Europe
text:
Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) are illnesses patients acquire during hospital care. They extend hospital stays, increase costs, and can lead to disability or death. Although Germany’s hospitals report lower infection prevalence than the EU/EEA average, the population-level burden is higher, largely due to high admission rates and longer lengths of stay.
This post compares Germany’s HAI burden with the EU/EEA and highlights which infection types matter most.

title: Germany’s Hidden Burden: Healthcare-Associated Infections Compared to Europe
text:
Data description
Source. European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC) Point Prevalence Survey, 2011–2012.
Focus. Five major HAIs that represent ~80% of cases: CDI, HAP, BSI, UTI, and SSI.

Cleaning & modelling (high level)
Estimates convert hospital prevalence to incidence and burden (DALYs) using the BCoDE approach, implemented in the open-source BHAI R package. Key adjustments: infection duration, outcomes/comorbidities, and uncertainty.
